1:
The witnesses divide along a familiar seam here. The grammatical-historical commentators spend their energy on the bare data of the verse — which feast, and when. John's vague "after these things" (so Robertson) is the evangelist's habit (John 3:22; John 6:1) of supplementing the Synoptics rather than narrating in tight sequence. Lightfoot notes that John alone marks all the Passovers from the baptism to the death (John 2:13; John 6:4; John 13:1), and reckons this unnamed feast a second Passover. Ellicott and Barnes are more cautious: the reading "a feast" (anarthrous) is the better one, and since the text refuses to name it, the identification must remain uncertain.

The Reformed and Wesleyan voices weigh the same chronology to an opposite conclusion. Calvin, tracing the order from the Passover of chapter 2 through Samaria and Galilee, judges this to be Pentecost; Wesley flatly glosses it "Pentecost," and Gill canvasses all three pilgrim-feasts before leaning the other way to Passover. The Puritan Henry lifts the devotional point above the dispute: Christ, "made under the law," kept the feast as a subject would, teaching us to attend the assemblies (Hebrews 10:25), and seized the gathered crowds as an opportunity for good.

The Greek and Latin fathers, gathered in the Catena, read the whole Bethesda scene typologically. The Antiochene Chrysostom hears in Jesus' pilgrimage a proof that He was no enemy of the Law but its observer, and treats the pool as a lesser type of baptism: the angel troubling the water prefigures the Spirit's grace, while the cure of only one at a time was meant to fix men's minds on water's healing power and lead them from the body's cure to the soul's. The Latin fathers, with Augustine, allegorize boldly — the water is the Jewish people (as the Apocalypse uses water for peoples, Revelation 17:15), the five porches are the five books of Moses that expose the impotent without curing them, and the man's thirty-eight years fall two short of forty, the number of the Law's perfection, the deficit being love's two commandments. Bede adds the moral: "sheep pool" names the people (Psalm 100:3), and the bidding to rise and walk is a summons out of torpor into good works.

So the one live debate is calendrical — Pentecost (Calvin, Wesley, with Chrysostom) against Passover (Lightfoot, Henry) against the agnostic verdict of Ellicott and Barnes — while Augustine's elaborate numerology stands out as the boldest reach beyond the letter.
2:
This is a verse for the grammatical-historical guild, and they dominate it. With near unanimity they correct the rendering: not "sheep-market" but the sheep-gate, the gate of Nehemiah 3:1 (cf. 3:32; 12:39) through which sacrificial animals were driven — the word "market" being absent from the text and unknown to Jewish sources (Gill, Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson). Robertson supplies the elided noun ("gate") and parses the rare word for pool (a swimming-bath, only here in the New Testament). On the name, the consensus reads Bethesda as "house of mercy."

The same school presses one disputed point of date. Robertson, with Wesley and Clarke, hears in the present tense "there is" a sign that John wrote before Jerusalem's destruction — though Robertson tempers it: this may be only "John's vivid memory," and Clarke notes the pool or its successor was still shown to travellers long after the city fell. The Wesleyan Clarke also flags a textual variant in the name.

The Reformed Calvin lifts the topographical detail to a theological purpose: the crowded, well-known site near the temple guarantees that the coming miracle "was not concealed or known to a few," and he dismisses Jerome's fanciful re-spelling of the name. Lightfoot, characteristically, mines the phrase "in the Hebrew tongue" to mean not classical Hebrew but the Chaldee or Aramaic the people had spoken since the return from Babylon.

Robertson adds the lexical detail that the pool is "surnamed" Bethesda — a present passive participle — and supplies the word pool as a rare term for a diving or swimming bath, found only here in the New Testament; the manuscripts vary between the forms Bethesda, Bethsaida, and Bethzatha, a fluidity Clarke also registers. Wesley, taking the name to mean "the house of mercy," infers from the five porches that "the basin had five sides." Beneath the topography the witnesses sense a deliberate purpose. Calvin makes the site's celebrity and its nearness to the temple part of the miracle's evidential force — the cure could be neither concealed nor known "to a few persons only." Even the disputed dating turns on a theological instinct: if John, writing decades later, still says the pool "is" there, the detail reflects either a survivor's vivid recollection (Robertson) or, with Wesley and Clarke, a Gospel composed while the landmark yet stood.
3:
The interest here is overwhelmingly textual, and the grammatical-historical commentators carry the verse. They agree on the plain sense first — a "great multitude of impotent folk," the blind, the lame, and the withered (the wasted limb of Matthew 12:10) lying in the porches; Barnes adds that the pool evidently had genuine medicinal, intermittent properties.

But the school splits sharply over the verse's closing words, "waiting for the moving of the water," which run straight into the whole of v.4. Ellicott and Robertson, following the oldest manuscripts (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) and the consensus of modern editors — Tischendorf, Tregelles, Alford, Westcott and Hort — judge the clause a later gloss, a "Western and Syrian addition" inserted to explain the troubling of the water in v.7. Against them stands the Wesleyan Clarke, who, while admitting the manuscript doubt, thinks the evidence insufficient to delete the words and keeps them. So the live debate is one of textual criticism: authentic narrative or explanatory interpolation.

Two voices read the multitude allegorically. The Reformed Gill makes the impotent folk an emblem of fallen men under the law of works — "blind, halt, and withered" in spiritual things, unable to keep the law or save themselves — while Calvin restrains the wonder, suggesting the sick lay there partly to beg of temple-goers, and that God cured a fixed number at each feast to commend the worship of the sanctuary.

Robertson presses the grammar a step further: the verb lay is an imperfect, and the singular form attaches to the collective noun multitude, while withered is the old adjective for a wasted, dried limb. He and Ellicott marshal the same editors against the closing clause — Tischendorf, Tregelles, Alford, Westcott and Hort — judging "waiting for the moving of the water" a Western and Syrian addition that anticipates v.7. Barnes notes that the three named classes — the blind, the lame, the withered — were those "least able to help themselves into the water," and so "lay longest" in the porches, a detail the Reformed Gill turns into a portrait of fallen man, who can neither keep the law nor "begin and carry on a work of grace upon" his own soul. Calvin, more soberly, will not let the wonder run unchecked, reminding us the Evangelist records only what he saw.
4:
This verse — the angel descending to trouble the water — is itself textually contested, and the witnesses split over both its authenticity and its meaning. On the text, the grammatical-historical Robertson is decisive: the whole verse is wanting in the oldest and best manuscripts (Aleph B C D W, the Old Syriac, the Coptic, the Vulgate) and is "undoubtedly added," like the clause in v.3, to clarify v.7; Tertullian is the earliest to mention it. Clarke counters that the addition "of the Lord" to "angel" is ancient and respectable enough to overthrow the rationalizing theory.

The deeper division is over angelic agency. The bare-supernatural reading is held by Gill and Clarke, who reject Dr. Hammond's notion that the "angel" was merely a messenger from the Sanhedrin and the cure natural; the healing was an act of God by angelic ministry. Barnes occupies a mediating grammatical-historical position: the angel need not have been seen — the people judged by the effect — and the spring may well have had strong natural medicinal properties, the Jews habitually ascribing all favours to angels (Hebrews 1:14; Genesis 19:15). The Wesleyan Wesley grants that "many undoubtedly thought the whole thing to be purely natural," and reads the periodic descent as God's way of reviving expectation that His people were not forgotten.

The Reformed Calvin turns the angelic ministry into doctrine: God employs angels not because He needs them but to display His power through them (Colossians 1:16), and it is "wicked and shameful" to ascribe to the creature what belongs to God alone.

The Wesleyan voices add their own observations to the angel debate. Wesley notes that the Greek "implies that he had ceased going down, before the time of St. John's writing," and reads the periodic descent as a sign meant "to raise expectation of the acceptable time approaching" and to show "that his ancient people were not entirely forgotten." Gill devotes his comment to demolishing Dr. Hammond's theory that the "angel" was a Sanhedrin messenger and the water medicinal from the washing of sacrifices, calling it "a strange conceit." Barnes, holding the mediating line, lists the texts in which the Jews "attributed all favours to the ministry of the angels" (Genesis 19:15; Acts 7:53; Galatians 3:19). So beneath the textual and natural-versus-supernatural disputes lies a shared conviction, voiced by Calvin: whatever the means, the healing was God's, and the creature must not be credited with what belongs to Him.
5:
The verse is brief and the comment thin, but a clear consensus forms around the man's thirty-eight years of infirmity as the measure of the miracle's greatness. The grammatical-historical voices fix the sense precisely: the number marks neither his age nor his time at the pool but the duration of the disease (Ellicott, Robertson) — half a human lifetime spent "helpless and friendless," so that he alone of those healed is addressed first and unasked. Barnes adds that the malady, whatever it was, disabled him from walking and was doubtless held incurable.

The Wesleyan Clarke notes Chrysostom's guess that the man was blind, but settles on palsy, and draws the pastoral point that poverty and disease were here joined; the long term makes the cure "the greater," ruling out all collusion. The Reformed Calvin reads the verse as the Evangelist's deliberate piling-up of circumstances that "prove that the miracle may be relied on as certain" — the hopeless duration, the lack of any helper, the repeated failed attempts — each detail magnifying the power of Christ. Gill simply anchors the scene at one of the five porches.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the pathos and the certainty the number secures. Ellicott observes that the man had "spent half the lot of human life in that condition," and that of all whom Christ healed at the pool, he alone is approached "of His own accord" and addressed first. Robertson renders the phrase with bare literalness — "having thirty and eight years," that is, "having spent thirty and eight years" in the infirmity. Clarke records Chrysostom's conjecture that the malady was blindness but settles on palsy, and notes that the very length of the affliction "makes the miracle of his cure the greater" and rules out all suspicion of collusion. The Reformed Calvin gathers these strands into a single point: "the long duration of the disease had taken away all hope of its being cured," and the absence of any helper "causes the power of Christ to be more strikingly displayed" — every circumstance the Evangelist names is calculated to prove the cure beyond dispute.
6:
The question "Wilt thou be made whole?" draws the witnesses to a single agreed point — Christ did not ask for information — and to two distinct emphases on why He asked. The grammatical-historical Robertson presses the grammar ("he has already been having much time," a progressive present) and leaves open how Jesus "knew" the man's long case — supernatural knowledge (John 2:24), observation, or overheard report.

The Reformed Calvin and the Wesleyan Clarke converge on the pastoral purpose: the question was meant to kindle desire for the favour offered and to rouse the attention of the witnesses, lest a sudden cure pass unnoticed. Clarke turns it into a homiletic axiom — addresses of this kind are always proper from gospel preachers, to stir hardened and desponding sinners alike to "desire and expect salvation." Lightfoot, ever the grammatical-historical contextualist, hears in the question a deliberate edge against sabbath scruple — "Wouldst thou be healed on the sabbath day?" — given how superstitious the rulers were on that point (Mark 3:2; Luke 13:14).

Two voices stand apart in scale. The Antiochene Chrysostom opens his homily not on the question but on the "all-sufficient" profit of the Scriptures themselves (Romans 15:4; 1 Corinthians 10:11) — they are a "treasury of all manner of medicines." And Ellicott alone strikes the somber note that this man's own sin had left its mark on him, a thread he will pick up at v.14.

The witnesses fill out both the homiletic and the pastoral dimensions of the question. The Antiochene Chrysostom, opening his homily on the "all-sufficient" aid of Scripture, frames the whole episode as a treasury "of all manner of medicines" for the soul. The Reformed Calvin names his two reasons precisely: the question was put "partly in order to kindle in the man a desire of the favor," and "partly to quicken the attention of the witnesses" who, distracted, might otherwise "not have perceived the miracle." Clarke turns it into a model of evangelical address — meant to stir "the hearts, as well of hardened as of desponding sinners" to "desire and expect salvation." Robertson notes the contrast with cases where faith is demanded first, as of the blind man: here the lame man "could not well know who He was," so the demand for faith is reserved until after the miracle. The question, then, is no idle inquiry but the opening of a cure.
7:
The impotent man's reply — "Sir, I have no man" — draws a shared reading: it is the voice of helplessness, not of unwillingness. The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene precisely. Robertson explains the popular belief behind the words: at each outflow of the intermittent spring the healing virtue was thought to belong to the first one in, so the man's whole hope was to be thrown in quickly (v.4). Barnes reads the answer as an implicit yes — he did wish it, but, added to all his trials, he had no friend to help. Ellicott raises this to pathos: there is "an eloquence of helplessness more powerful than that of words," the man day by day watching and listening for the bubbling water.

The Reformed Calvin makes the verse a mirror of our own faith. The man "limits the assistance of God according to his own thought," daring to expect no more than his mind can conceive — exactly what almost all of us do, fixing on the means within reach. Yet Christ "forgives his weakness," and in that forbearance every believer has daily experience of grace meeting us below our deserving. The Wesleyan voices add smaller notes: Wesley sees him simply giving the reason he was not yet whole, while Clarke records the variant "Yea, Lord, but I have no man" and turns to thankfulness for health and the misery of poverty joined to long affliction.

Robertson supplies the grammar of the man's hope: the indefinite temporal clause "when the water is troubled" and the final clause "that he throw me in" capture the popular belief that healing power rested in the spring for the first to enter at each intermittent outflow. The Reformed Calvin presses the mirror further — the man's fault is ours, for "we keep our attention fixed on the means which are within our reach" and venture to expect from God no more than we can already conceive; yet the verse is also "a mirror of that forbearance" by which Christ meets our small faith. The Puritan note sounds quietly through the others: though disappointed time and again, the man "still continued lying by the pool side," a patience under long affliction the gospel commends (cf. Habakkuk 2:3). What looks like the end of hope is, the witnesses agree, exactly the moment Christ chooses to act.
8:
Over the command "Rise, take up thy bed, and walk," the schools agree on the deity it displays and differ only in idiom. The Wesleyan Clarke states the consensus most sharply: "Jesus speaks here as God. He speaks in no name but his own, and with an authority which belongs to God alone" — and the instant, total cure is an incontestable proof of His omnipotence. The grammatical-historical Robertson confirms it from the grammar, parsing the three imperatives (a sort of exclamation, "Get up," then pick up the pallet, then "go on walking") and the colloquial word for the poor man's pallet (Mark 2:11). Barnes stresses that the command to carry the bed was itself evidence the miracle was real, not imagined.

Two of the same school turn to the sabbath storm the command will raise. Lightfoot lays out the rabbinic distinction between carrying within a private place and into the public domain, so that the bed-bearing was a deliberate, visible breach of their tradition (cf. Jeremiah 17:21). Ellicott observes that there is no formal demand of faith here, because faith is a "living principle," the seeing of Him who is invisible (Hebrews 11:27).

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem supplies the one distinctly patristic note: the name Jesus means "Saviour" in Hebrew and "Healer" in Greek, and here He ministers first to the soul and then to the body — for the body had been "palsied for the sin of the soul," a thread that ties this command to the warning of v.14, "Sin no more."

Lightfoot extends the sabbath frame with rabbinic precision, distinguishing what may be carried within a "private place" from what is borne into the "public" domain, so that the bed-bearing was a calculated, visible breach of their tradition. Barnes underlines how "remarkable" the command was — addressed to one disabled for nearly forty years — and how the carrying of the bed served as public proof of a real, instantaneous cure. Clarke notes Christ's habit, after a miracle, of attaching "some circumstance... which attested its truth." The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem holds the deeper order in view: because "the body was palsied for the sin of the soul," Christ "ministered first to the soul that He might extend the healing to the body," joining the command of this verse to the warning "Sin no more" of v.14. The single word of Christ, the witnesses agree, both confers the cure and proves it.
9:
The instant cure — "immediately the man was made whole" — and the ominous closing note, "and on the same day was the sabbath," set the witnesses onto the controversy now opening. The grammatical-historical Robertson marks both the precise tenses (he took up the pallet at once, then went on walking) and the larger significance: this is "the first of the violations of the Sabbath rules" by Jesus in Jerusalem, the seed of a bitterness that will run on (John 9:14, 16). Barnes faces the obvious objection — why command on the sabbath what the Jews held unlawful (Jeremiah 17:21; Nehemiah 13:15) — and answers that the Son of man is Lord of the sabbath (Matthew 12:8), with the right to declare and even dispense with such a rule; Clarke reasons to the same end.

The Reformed Calvin presses the point of design: Christ knew well "how great offense would immediately arise," yet deliberately chose this public display so the miracle might be widely known and so He might "open up the way" for the great discourse to follow (vv.17–47). Ellicott notes only that the cure was in "the striking form that none could gainsay."

Robertson presses the significance of the timing: this sabbath cure is "the first of the violations of the Sabbath rules of the Jews by Jesus in Jerusalem," and "this controversy will" deepen, as the parallels in the man born blind show (John 9:14, 16). Barnes weighs the obvious difficulty — that Christ bade a man do on the sabbath what the law seemed to forbid — and answers from the Lord's authority over the day and from the poor man's circumstance, having no house to which to carry the bed. The Reformed Calvin assigns Christ a double design: "that the miracle might be more extensively known," and that He might "open up the way for the beautiful discourse" of vv.17–47. The instantaneous passage from helpless weakness to a strength able to bear a burden is, for all the witnesses, the proof that nothing "is too hard for Christ to do."
10:
The Jews' challenge — "it is not lawful for thee to carry thy bed" — exposes their priorities, and the witnesses converge on that exposure. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the legal frame: carrying burdens was held unlawful on the sabbath (Exodus 23:12; Nehemiah 13:19; Jeremiah 17:21), the rabbinical penalty stoning (Robertson), so that to them "the healing of the man was a minor detail." Ellicott sharpens the irony: they cavil at what they cannot deny, murmuring at the bed while a once-palsied man walks before them.

The Reformed Calvin renders a careful verdict: in maintaining the sanctity of the sabbath the Jews "justly and properly accuse the man" — but they fall into fault the moment his explanation fails to satisfy them, for once the reason was known he ought to have been acquitted; and the One who laid the burden on his shoulders "discharges him by his own authority." The Puritan Henry, reaching ahead to the warning of v.14, presses the devotional lesson that those eased of sin's punishment are in danger of returning to sin once "the terror and restraint are over," unless divine grace dries up the fountain. Cyril of Jerusalem, more loosely, uses the scene to set the servant beneath the Son of God.

Robertson adds the legal sharpness the others imply: the rabbinical penalty for sabbath burden-bearing was stoning, and the rulers' fixation on the pallet rather than the restored limbs shows that "the healing of the man was a minor detail" to them. Ellicott presses the moral inversion — one might have expected "wonder and thankfulness that the man could walk at all," but the formalism that had "well nigh crushed all heart out of them" produced only a murmur about the law. The Reformed Calvin holds the balance with care: in principle the duty of sabbath-keeping was theirs to defend, and they "justly and properly accuse the man" — yet justice required that, the reason once known, "he ought to have been acquitted," and the One who laid on the burden "discharges him by his own authority." Cyril of Jerusalem, more loosely, makes the episode an occasion to exalt the Son above every servant of God.
11:
The healed man's defense — "He that made me whole, the same said unto me, Take up thy bed" — is praised across the witnesses as sound reasoning. The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan voices agree on its logic: Clarke calls it conclusive — "He who could work such a miracle must be at least the best of men," and a good man commands no evil; Barnes draws the wider rule that the One with power to heal has the right to explain the law, and that mercy received "imposes the obligation to obey." Robertson notes the man did not so much as know Jesus' name, yet quotes His very words.

Ellicott distills the same point theologically: the man "bases the use of his power upon the will of Him who had given it" — that divine voice is the one authority he has heard, and it cannot be wrong to obey it. The cure, in other words, carries its own warrant for the command. (Cyril of Jerusalem's lines attached here run off to the descent of the Spirit at the Lord's baptism and bear only loosely on the verse.)

Clarke states the man's logic as a syllogism: "He who could work such a miracle must be at least the best of men: now a good man will neither do evil himself, nor command others to do it," therefore the bed-carrying can be no sin. Barnes broadens it into a principle of obligation — the One with "power to work so signal a miracle" has "a right to explain the law," and a kindness so great "imposes the obligation to obey." Robertson notes the emphatic demonstrative — "that one" made me whole — and that the man, still ignorant even of Jesus' name, nonetheless "quotes the very words of Jesus," resting his whole defense upon them. Ellicott distils the theology: the man "bases the use of his power upon the will of Him who had given it," the one divine voice he has heard, which it "cannot be wrong for him to obey." The cure, in short, is its own warrant for the command.
12:
The question "What man is that which said unto thee, Take up thy bed?" is read by every witness as a self-betrayal. The grammatical-historical commentators catch its tone and its omission. Robertson hears contempt — "Who is the fellow?" — and notes they ask only about the command to break the sabbath, not about the healing. Barnes calls it "a remarkable instance of the perverseness of men": instead of the miracle they fasten on the supposed violation, assuming guilt and meditating vengeance. Ellicott makes the same observation structurally — "they pass over the giving of the power, and quote only the command" that falls under their technical prohibition; the real motive is a charge against Christ, whose power the nation was feeling "as a life-current."

The unanimity is striking: across the school, the question proves that the rulers cared nothing for the restored man and everything for an indictment. Cyril of Jerusalem again returns to the etymology of Jesus as Healer of soul and body, the only voice lifting the eye above the controversy.

The school is strikingly of one mind here. Barnes calls the question "a remarkable instance of the perverseness of men," who, assuming "as certain that nothing could make his conduct... proper," at once "meditated vengeance." Ellicott reads the same omission as deliberate: "the life and strength of once-palsied limb is as nothing; the fact that this man was breaking their tradition is secondary," for "the real motive is a charge against Him" whose power the nation was feeling "as a life-current, quickening deadened energies." Robertson hears in the very phrasing — "Who is the fellow?" — a contempt that asks only about "the command to violate the Sabbath, not about the healing." Against this unanimous portrait of malice, Cyril of Jerusalem alone lifts the eye, returning to the name of Jesus as the "physician of souls and bodies" who guides "sinners' steps to repentance" — the very thing the rulers, intent on their indictment, refuse to see.

Gill notes the rulers were already "suspecting who had made him whole," and asked not in wonder but to fix the deed on its author; the question is thus the first move of a settled design against Christ, in which the restored man counts for nothing and the broken tradition for everything.
13:
That "he that was healed wist not who it was, for Jesus had conveyed himself away," occupies the witnesses with both a grammatical nicety and a theological purpose. The grammatical-historical Robertson dwells on the rare verb — to slip out, or to turn the head aside — by which Jesus withdrew unobserved. Ellicott corrects a common misreading: the clause does not mean a crowd gathered round the miracle (which would imply the man knew Jesus); the better rendering is that Jesus simply disappeared "among the multitude that was in that place." Clarke agrees that He withdrew because His work was not yet finished and He would not expose Himself to the rulers' malice.

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrinal point that the others only imply: Christ withdrew so that "the Jews might have it in their power to judge of the fact itself, without reference to any person" — and hence, he insists, "the cure of this man cannot be ascribed to his faith," since even after being healed he does not yet acknowledge his Healer. The miracle's evidence is to rest on the work, not on the recipient's belief.

Robertson lingers on the rare verb — it may come from a root meaning to swim out or to turn the head aside — by which Jesus "slipped out" of the press unnoticed. Clarke explains the withdrawal practically: Christ "had not yet finished his work, and would not expose himself to the envy and malice of the Jewish rulers." Ellicott corrects the popular impression that a crowd had gathered round the cure, since on that reading the man "must have known who He was"; the better sense is that Jesus simply "disappeared among" the multitude already present. The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrinal weight: Christ withdrew so the Jews could "judge of the fact itself, without reference to any person," and therefore "the cure of this man cannot be ascribed to his faith," for even after it he does not yet know his Healer. The miracle's testimony, all agree, is meant to rest on the work and not on the recipient.
14:
Christ's words in the temple — "Sin no more, lest a worse thing come unto thee" — unite the witnesses on the link between this man's sin and his suffering, while they weigh how far that link reaches. The Antiochene Chrysostom states the principle most strongly: sin is "fearful, and the ruin of the soul," and because we feel no pain when the soul is diseased, God often "punisheth the body for the transgressions of the soul." The grammatical-historical Robertson confirms the grammar — "no longer go on sinning" — a "clear implication that disease was due to personal sin, as is so often the case," but immediately qualifies it: not all sickness is so caused (John 9:3), and these are the same words later spoken in the disputed passage of John 8:11.

The Latin fathers gathered in the Catena read the setting devotionally: the man, once healed, did not run to the marketplace or to pleasure but went to the temple — and there, in the sacred place, he recognizes Jesus whom he had not known in the crowd; Alcuin draws the lesson that to "attain to the sight of" our Maker we must "avoid the crowd." Barnes and Ellicott press the same gratitude — the first use of restored power was thanksgiving (cf. Isaiah 38:20) — Ellicott adding that to be freed from the effect is to be summoned to be freed from the cause.

The Reformed Calvin and the Wesleyan Wesley close the circle: affliction is God's discipline against sin, and the warning means we "make an improper use of the gifts of God" if recovery does not issue in new obedience. So the one open debate is the reach of the principle — a real connection of sin and sickness (Chrysostom, Wesley), held without becoming a rule (Robertson's caveat from 9:3).

The Catena's Latin fathers add a moral geography to the scene. Augustine marks that the man "does not recognize Jesus in the crowd; but in the temple, being a sacred place, he does," and Alcuin draws the rule that to "attain to the sight of Him, we must avoid the crowd." Chrysostom presses the principle of providence — because "when the soul is diseased we feel no pain," God in mercy "oftentimes punisheth the body for the transgressions of the soul," that the visible scourge may drive us to the invisible cure. Barnes turns it to thanksgiving: those "raised up from a bed of pain" ought, like Hezekiah (Isaiah 38:20), to "seek the sanctuary of God and give him thanks." Robertson's caution remains the governing balance — the present imperative "sin no more" implies a real link of this man's suffering to his sin, yet John 9:3 forbids making it a universal law.
15:
The man's report — "the man departed, and told the Jews that it was Jesus" — divides the witnesses into a genuine debate over his motive. The grammatical-historical Robertson reads it darkly: instead of heeding the warning, "he went off and told the Jews," and was "either ungrateful and wilfully betrayed Jesus" or sought to clear himself with the ecclesiastics and escape a possible stoning. The accent falls on betrayal.

Against this, the Reformed Calvin defends the man's innocence: "nothing was farther from his intention than to make Christ an object of their hatred," and nothing farther from his expectation than their fury; his purpose was "pious," wishing to render his Physician the honour justly due. The Wesleyan Clarke supports a charitable reading — the man told them not that Jesus ordered the bed-carrying but that Jesus had healed him, leaving them to draw the inference — and Wesley turns the verse against the Jews rather than the man: one might have expected crowds to throng the Healer, but instead "they surround him with a hostile intent." Ellicott, more neutrally, finds nothing in the narrative suggesting defiance: the man simply now gave the answer he could not give at v.13.

So the verse is genuinely contested: a careless or self-serving betrayal (Robertson) against a well-meant, if naive, act of honour (Calvin, Clarke, Wesley, Ellicott).

Ellicott resists reading defiance into the report: the narrative "does not suggest that he did this in a tone of defiance," only that he now supplied "the answer which he could not give before" at v.13, perhaps to clear himself of the charge of sabbath-breaking. Clarke reads him charitably — he reported the cure, not the command — while Robertson keeps the darker possibility open, that he "wilfully betrayed Jesus" to the authorities to "escape a possible stoning." The Reformed Calvin defends his intention as wholly "pious": he "wished to render to his Physician the honor which was justly due," and "nothing was farther from his expectation than that they would rage so furiously." Wesley turns the lens from the man to the rulers, whose response was the true scandal: rather than throng the Healer to "receive the blessings of the Gospel," they "conspire against his life... for an imagined transgression in point of ceremony."
16:
The verse states the turn — "therefore did the Jews persecute Jesus, because he had done these things on the sabbath" — and the witnesses, almost wholly grammatical-historical here, attend to its grammar and its text. Robertson presses the tenses: "began to persecute" (inchoative imperfect) and "because he was doing" (imperfect), so the charge is not one act but a settled pattern — Jesus "was becoming a regular Sabbath-breaker" in their eyes, and now they had cause for an open breach after earlier friction (John 2:18; 4:1). Ellicott reads the same imperfect — "He was doing" — and infers either that they generalized a rule of practice from this one instance or that earlier unrecorded miracles were also wrought on the sabbath (cf. Luke 6:1–11).

On the text, Clarke and Ellicott note that the clause "and sought to slay him" is absent from several manuscripts and probably inserted from v.18 — though Clarke judges it true of the Jews whether original or not. Barnes draws the moral that "every bigot and persecutor" assumes the accused wrong rather than inquiring whether he had proved himself the Messiah.

The grammatical-historical reading rests on the force of the imperfect. Robertson: "began to persecute" and kept it up, taking this cure as "one excuse," their earlier suspicion (John 2:18; 4:1) now hardening into an open breach, so that "the Pharisees will watch his conduct on the Sabbath henceforth." Ellicott reads "He was doing" as "expressing continuance or custom," and infers either that they generalised a settled charge from this instance or that earlier, unrecorded sabbath miracles lay behind it (cf. Luke 6:1–11). On the disputed clause "and sought to slay him," Clarke notes it is omitted by good manuscripts and was probably drawn in from v.18, though he judges the sentiment true regardless. Barnes lifts the abiding lesson: "every bigot and persecutor" assumes the accused wrong rather than asking whether he had given "proof that he was the Messiah."

Gill adds that the persecution was at first "with their tongues, reproaching and reviling him" as a sabbath-breaker and "a destroyer of the law," before it hardened into the design upon His life; the verse thus marks the hinge at which suspicion becomes settled hostility, the sabbath cure serving merely as its pretext.
17:
Christ's defense — "My Father worketh hitherto, and I work" — is heard by all the witnesses as a claim of unique Sonship and ceaseless divine activity, and the schools reinforce one another. The grammatical-historical commentators do the close work: Robertson stresses that He says "My Father," not "our Father" — a claim to a "peculiar relation" — and that the present tense means God "keeps on working until now" without a sabbath break, a fact noted by Philo, Justin Martyr, and Origen. Barnes and Clarke supply the theology of providence: God rested from creating on the seventh day (Genesis 2:2) but never from governing — He makes the sun rise and the grass grow on the sabbath — so the command to rest does not bind the Creator. Ellicott cites the contemporary Philo, "to work is the property of God," and roots the defense in Christ's "Sonship and unity with that God."

The Reformed Calvin clarifies the strategy: Christ does not argue that the sabbath law was temporary and now abolished, but that He "has not violated the Law, because this is a divine work" — though he grants that the ceremonial sabbath was "a part of the shadows of the Law" which Christ's coming ended. The Wesleyan Wesley reads the same uninterrupted labour "from the creation till now," and treats the verse as the proposition the rest of the discourse will expound (vv.19–30). Lightfoot grounds it in Jewish idiom — before the Sanhedrin Christ asserts both that the Messiah is God and that He is the Messiah — and Cyril of Jerusalem turns it against those "heretics who sever the Old Testament from the New," worshipping the Maker as the Father of Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators ground the claim in the doctrine of providence: Barnes and Clarke insist that God, having "rested from creating" on the seventh day (Genesis 2:2), "never ceased from preserving and governing," for "nothing can continue to exist" without His sustaining work — and so "he can keep no sabbaths." Robertson notes the linear present, "keeps on working until now," a continuity of divine activity that Philo, Justin Martyr, and Origen all remarked. Lightfoot reads the saying as a double claim before the Sanhedrin — "the Son of God" and "the Messiah" being convertible terms — anchored in the confession of Matthew 16:16. The Reformed Calvin clarifies the strategy: Christ does not plead that the sabbath law is abolished, but that "he has not violated the Law, because this is a divine work," the ceremonial sabbath being a shadow that His coming fulfils rather than breaks.
18:
The Jews' inference — that by calling God "his own Father" Jesus was "making himself equal with God" — is endorsed by every witness as a correct reading of His claim. The Wesleyan Wesley puts it most pointedly: the phrase means "his own Father in such a sense as no creature can"; the hearers all so understood Him, "and our Lord never contradicted, but confirmed it." Clarke agrees that the Jews "did not take a wrong meaning out of our Lord's words" — He plainly made Himself one with God — and now they had two pretexts for His death: sabbath-breaking and blasphemy. Ellicott notes it was "a claim which none other had ever made," that God was in a peculiar sense His own Father, and so a claim to divinity.

The grammatical-historical Robertson tracks the rising violence — "sought the more" (imperfect) is a "graphic picture of increased and untiring effort," a murderous wrath that John will repeat (John 7:1, 19; 8:37) and that will only grow. Barnes and the Reformed Calvin draw the lesson from Christ's refusal to soften the claim: He added what He knew would enrage them because "we are not to keep back truth" (Barnes), and by His example taught that "we ought never to yield to the fury of wicked men" but maintain God's truth (Calvin). The deity of Christ, on this verse, is held without dissent — and held precisely because Jesus owned rather than retracted the charge.

The witnesses agree that the Jews' inference was exact, and that Christ's silence in the face of it is itself a confession. Clarke notes they had now found "two pretenses to take away his life" — that He had "dissolved" the sabbath and that He was "guilty of blasphemy" in making Himself one with God. Ellicott corrects the tense — "did He break" and "His own Father" — and stresses that the claim was unprecedented: "none other had ever made" God his Father "in a peculiar sense." Robertson traces the swelling violence with John's recurring refrain, "they sought to kill him" (John 7:1, 19, 25; 8:37), a wrath that, if this is a Passover, will "grow for two years." The Reformed Calvin draws the example for the church — Christ "intended... to make a public display of their incurable malice," teaching that we "ought never to yield to the fury of wicked men."
19:
This verse — "The Son can do nothing of himself, but what he seeth the Father do" — became the great Arian battleground, and the witnesses close ranks against the heretical reading while parsing the orthodox one. The Reformed Calvin states the issue directly: Christ "is so far from vindicating himself" against the Jews' charge of equality that "he maintains more openly that it is true"; the words describe the inseparable working of Father and Son, not the Son's inferiority, and Calvin judges that the ancients need not have been so distressed refuting "the calumny of Arius," who urged this text against the Son's equality. The whole Latin and Eastern patristic tradition agrees: Hilary hears Christ asserting "the excellency of His birth and nature," what He does being "in reality His Father's doing, who acted in the Son"; Augustine makes the operation of Father and Son "inseparable" — the Father doing all things through the Son as through His Word (John 1:3); and Cyril of Jerusalem reads the dependence as the Son "honouring His own Father."

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan voices secure the same point exegetically. Robertson notes the absolute use of "the Son," admitting the charge of v.18 and defending the equality — true in a sense of every man, but "in a much deeper sense of Christ" (cf. John 8:28; 14:10). Clarke argues from the mutuality — neither can the Father "do any thing of himself, because of his infinite unity with the Son" — that one who does "whatsoever God does" is "no created being." Wesley frames it most memorably: this inability "is not his imperfection, but his glory, resulting from his eternal, intimate, indissoluble unity with the Father." Lightfoot adds the Jewish counterpart, reading "the Son" as "the Messiah" the sent Servant (Isaiah 42:1), and Ellicott warns that where the Jews saw blasphemy, "men have since seen... inferiority" — and both miss the relation of Father and Son that is the key.

The Latin Augustine, in his tractate, sets the interpretive key the whole tradition follows: John, who "reclined" on the Lord's breast, was given to "utter those things concerning the Son of God" that rouse but do not fill the little ones, and the verse must be read so that "the Father doeth not some, the Son others, but... the Father doeth all things through the Son, as through His Word" (John 1:3). Gill, with the Reformed, observes that Christ does not deny the charge of blasphemy but "vindicates himself," allowing the whole and explaining it. Barnes traces the careful sequence — having claimed equality, Christ now "proceeds... to show its source and nature," that the Son does "nothing without the appointment of the Father; nothing contrary to the Father." So the consensus stands firm against the Arian misreading: the Son's "can do nothing of himself" names not a lesser nature but the unbroken unity of will and operation between Father and Son.
20:
On "the Father loveth the Son, and showeth him all things," the witnesses agree that the verse grounds Christ's authority in an eternal love and an unreserved revelation. The grammatical-historical commentators carry the exposition. Barnes presses the disclosure — the Father "conceals nothing from him," whereas from prophets and philosophers much is hidden — and identifies the promised "greater works" as the raising of the dead and the judgment of the world. Ellicott makes the relation reciprocal: the Son's relation implies a "moral inability to do anything apart from the Father," the Father's relation a "moral necessity to impart all to the Son," the whole resting on "oneness of essence." Robertson alone pauses on the philology, noting that the love here is named with one Greek verb and at John 3:35 with another, and declining to force a distinction between them.

The Latin Augustine keeps the doctrine of inseparable operation in view — the Father does all things through the Son as through His Word (John 1:3). The Reformed Calvin parts company with the older fathers' gloss that "God loves himself in the Son," calling it "harsh and far-fetched"; he applies the love rather to Christ "clothed with flesh," the beloved in whom the whole love of God dwells (Matthew 3:17). The Wesleyan Wesley draws the unity tightest: "at the same time (not at different times) the Father showeth and doth, and the Son seeth and doth," and the greater works are those at which they marvelled when He raised Lazarus. The Wesleyan Clarke adds his recurring caution that Christ speaks "sometimes as God, and sometimes as the ambassador of God," a distinction his two natures require.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the totality of the disclosure. Barnes contrasts the Son with prophets and philosophers, from whom "no small part of the doings of God are concealed," whereas "from the Son nothing is." Ellicott reasons from the relation itself: as Sonship implies a "moral inability to do anything apart from the Father," so Fatherhood implies a "moral necessity to impart all to the Son," and the promised "greater works" can "only be explained" by this oneness of essence. Robertson weighs the two Greek verbs for love and declines to drive a wedge between them, both naming "the mystery of love of the Father for the Son." The Reformed Calvin parts from the older gloss that "God loves himself in the Son," applying the love instead to Christ "clothed with flesh" (Matthew 3:17), and Wesley fixes the unity in time — the Father "showeth and doth," the Son "seeth and doth," together and not at intervals.
21:
The promise that "as the Father raiseth up the dead... even so the Son quickeneth whom he will" draws the witnesses to two questions: the kind of raising meant, and the sovereignty of the Son's will. The Latin Augustine, in the Catena, fixes the first point that the others develop: these are the "greater works," and we must not think "some are raised by the Father, others by the Son" — the Son raises the same dead the Father raises, by the same will.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators read the raising on two planes at once. Ellicott states the frame for the whole paragraph (vv.21–29): resurrection and judgment "regarded as spiritual as well as physical, as present as well as future," the work of the Father manifested by the Son. Gill makes the present-tense "quickeneth" the spiritual raising of dead sinners, then allows the natural sense. Barnes and Clarke anchor the literal power in the Old Testament precedents — the widow of Zarephath's son (1 Kings 17:22) and the Shunammite's (2 Kings 4:32–35) — which the Son will match in the ruler's daughter, the widow's son at Nain (Luke 7:11–15), and Lazarus (John 11:43–44). Robertson observes that Jesus claims this power "on a par with the power of the Father" before He has yet raised anyone.

On the sovereignty, the Reformed Calvin makes the Son "the Author of life," which contains "not only righteousness, but all the gifts of the Holy Spirit, and every part of our salvation"; the "whom he will" is read by Calvin and Ellicott as the free, life-giving choice of the Son, limited (Ellicott) only by human receptivity.

The witnesses agree the verse opens the "greater works" of v.20, and read the raising on a double plane. Gill takes the present tense "quickeneth" first of the spiritual raising of "dead sinners from the death of sin," then of the bodily. Barnes and Clarke supply the Old Testament warrant — that God's power to raise the dead was already shown through Elijah (1 Kings 17:22) and Elisha (2 Kings 4:32–35) — which the Son will exercise in His own name at Nain (Luke 7:11–15) and at the tomb of Lazarus (John 11:43–44). Robertson notes the boldness of the claim: "as yet, so far as we know, Jesus had not raised the dead," yet He claims the power "on a par with the power of the Father." The Reformed Calvin makes the life given comprehensive — "not only righteousness, but all the gifts of the Holy Spirit, and every part of our salvation."
22:
That "the Father judgeth no man, but hath committed all judgment unto the Son" is taken by every witness as a further proof of equality. The grammatical-historical Barnes lays out the cumulative argument of the discourse: Christ has claimed the same power over the sabbath (v.17), the same works (v.19), the same power to raise the dead (v.21), and now the same authority to judge — which "implies ability" to know all hearts. Robertson notes the perfect tense, "hath given," a settled state, and points to the picture of Christ as Judge in Matthew 25:31–46. Clarke reads it as confirming that "the Father acts not without the Son, nor the Son without the Father; their acts are common, their power equal."

Ellicott makes "to judge" the correlative opposite of "to quicken": the life given to some is itself a separation, a judgment, of the rest. The Reformed Calvin sets the verse in his governing image — the Father "governs the world in the person of the Son," having given Him a kingdom to rule heaven and earth at His pleasure, not surrendering His own authority but exercising it through the Son. The Wesleyan Wesley adds that the Father judges "not without the Son: but he doth judge by that man whom he hath ordained" (Acts 17:31), and Cyril of Jerusalem turns it to terror — God "needs not examination or proofs, to condemn," for the conscience will witness in the day He judges the secrets of men.

The grammatical-historical Barnes places the verse within the discourse's mounting argument for equality: Christ has claimed the same lordship over the sabbath, the same works, and the same power to raise the dead, and now the same authority to judge — a power that "implies ability" to "investigate the actions and thoughts of the life." Robertson marks the perfect tense, "hath given," a settled and abiding grant (as at John 3:35), and points to the great judgment scene of Matthew 25:31–46. Ellicott makes "to judge" the exact opposite of "to quicken": the life given to some in v.24 is itself "a separation from, and a judgment" upon the rest. Calvin meets the seeming difficulty — that the Father should "surrender his right" — by insisting He does not resign His authority but exercises it in the Son, having "given to the Son a kingdom" to govern heaven and earth.
23:
The purpose clause — "that all men should honour the Son, even as they honour the Father" — yields one of the discourse's plainest deity-arguments, and the witnesses are unanimous. The Wesleyan voices put it as a syllogism: if the Son is to be honoured "Even As the Father is honoured, then the Son must be God," for "to worship any creature is idolatry" (Clarke), and Wesley declares the verse "demonstrates the EQUALITY of the Son with the Father" — were He God only "by office or investiture," He could not be honoured with the same honour as the Father. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson agree: Jesus "claims here the same right to worship from men that the Father has," and dishonouring the Son is dishonouring the Father who sent Him (John 8:49; 1 John 2:23; cf. Luke 10:16).

The Reformed Calvin draws the practical corollary that anchors his whole reading of the chapter: because God "manifests his power and shows himself to be present" in Christ, "the Father wishes to be acknowledged and worshipped in the Son" — so our duty is to "seek God the Father in Christ." The Antiochene Chrysostom frames the surrounding warning soberly — we shall "render account" at the tribunal (2 Corinthians 5:10) — while Cyril of Jerusalem grounds the honour in the mutual knowledge of Father and Son: "the Scriptures also testify that He who was begotten is God."

The Wesleyan Clarke draws the inference into the open: "If then the Son is to be honored, Even As the Father is honored, then the Son must be God," for to receive "that worship which belongs to God alone" would, in a creature, be idolatry. Wesley makes the verse a proof text for the homoousion — were Christ God only "by office or investiture, and not in the unity of the Divine essence," He "could not be honoured even as... the Father." Robertson gathers John's repeated witness that "dishonouring Jesus is dishonouring the Father who sent him" (John 12:26; 15:23; 1 John 2:23). The Reformed Calvin secures the practical end: because the Father "wishes to be acknowledged and worshipped in the Son," our duty is "to seek God the Father in Christ" — there is no honouring of God that bypasses the Son.
24:
The solemn "He that heareth my word, and believeth on him that sent me, hath everlasting life... but is passed from death unto life" draws a near-unanimous reading: this is a present possession, secured now. The grammatical-historical commentators define the terms. Barnes restricts "heareth" to its full sense — to "receive in a proper manner... to obey" (cf. John 8:47), not the mere outward act. Robertson marks the perfect tense, "hath passed out of death into life," a completed transfer, and carefully distinguishes the spiritual life here from the physical resurrection of v.28. Ellicott reads "doth not come into judgment" (present) and finds the verse explaining the "whom he willeth" of v.21 — its only limit is "human receptivity."

The Latin Augustine, in the Catena, probes why one is to hear Christ's word yet believe the Father who sent Him — making faith "the step" and understanding "the fruit of faith." The Reformed Calvin warns against a false security: the gospel is Christ's "scepter," and "nothing is more common than a false profession of Christianity," so the hearing meant is a real subjection. At this point the Wesleyan Wesley enters his distinctive note: the believer "cometh not into condemnation — unless he make shipwreck of the faith" — a conditional accent the Reformed voices do not sound. Cyril of Jerusalem cites the verse to distinguish "dogmatic" faith, the soul's assent that secures everlasting life, and the Puritan Henry hears in it the voice of the Son raising those "dead in sin... to newness of life, by the power of the Spirit."

The Catena's Latin Augustine probes the ordering of faith and understanding — "the step to our piety is faith, the fruit of faith, understanding" — and the curious turn that one is to hear Christ's word yet believe the Father who sent Him, the unity of the two grounding the single act of trust. Robertson distinguishes the planes with care: in vv.24–25 Jesus speaks of spiritual life and death, in vv.28–29 of physical, and "one must notice carefully the quick transition"; the perfect "hath passed out of death into life" names a transfer already accomplished. The Reformed Calvin warns that the hearing meant is no "frivolous" outward profession — the gospel is Christ's "scepter" — while the Wesleyan Wesley adds his characteristic proviso, "unless he make shipwreck of the faith," a conditional note the Reformed assurance does not sound. Henry hears the Son's life-giving voice raising the spiritually dead "to newness of life, by the power of the Spirit."
25:
The double saying "The hour is coming, and now is, when the dead shall hear the voice of the Son of God" is read by the dominant body of witnesses as the spiritual resurrection, present already. The decisive clause is "and now is." The grammatical-historical Robertson is emphatic: this is "not the future resurrection in verse 28, but the spiritual resurrection here and now," the dead being the spiritually dead, dead in trespasses and sins (Ephesians 2:1, 5:14). Ellicott reasons the same way — the "now is" cannot apply to the bodily resurrection of v.28 — and Barnes finds the conversion of sinners under Christ's ministry. The Latin Augustine had already set the pattern in the Catena, taking "and now is" of the resurrection of the soul, with Chrysostom adding that the phrase signals how near it stands.

The Reformed Calvin grounds the certainty in Christ's oath — He "swears so frequently in reference to our salvation" because the truth seems incredible, that the very voice of the Son of God imparts life. The one discordant accent comes from the Wesleyan Wesley, who illustrates "the dead shall hear" with the bodily raisings — Jairus's daughter, the widow's son, Lazarus — and from Lightfoot, who records that the Jews expected the resurrection at Messiah's coming (from Ezekiel 37 and Isaiah 26:19) though they "grope exceedingly in the dark." So the debate is one of reference: the spiritual resurrection now (Augustine, Ellicott, Robertson, Barnes, Calvin) against the literal raisings that illustrate it (Wesley).

The decisive phrase remains "and now is." Robertson is categorical: this is "not the future resurrection in verse 28, but the spiritual resurrection here and now," the dead being those "dead in trespasses and sins" (Ephesians 2:1, 5:14) who already hear the Son's voice in conversion. Ellicott reasons that the clause "cannot be applied to the physical resurrection" of v.28 and that "live" must mean "the higher spiritual life." Barnes allows the words to "apply to either," but finds the primary reference in sinners "converted under his ministry." The Latin Augustine had already taken "now is" of the soul's resurrection, with Chrysostom adding that the phrase shows "it will not be long before it comes." The Reformed Calvin grounds the certainty in Christ's solemn oath, repeated "so frequently in reference to our salvation" because the truth — that a mere voice imparts life — seems "incredible" to unbelief.
26:
The ground of all the foregoing — "as the Father hath life in himself, so hath he given to the Son to have life in himself" — unites the witnesses on the Son's possession of self-existent life, while they differ over the moment of the "given." The grammatical-historical commentators define the phrase "in himself." Barnes: God is "the living God," in opposition to lifeless idols (Acts 14:15; Jeremiah 10:10), whose life is "not derived from any other being," unlike our derived life (Genesis 2:7). Robertson agrees that the Living God "possesses life wholly in himself" and has bestowed this power on the Son (John 1:3), and follows Westcott in taking the "as... so" to mark "the fact, not the degree."

The division concerns the "given." The Wesleyan Wesley reads it of eternal relation — life given to the Son "by eternal generation, to have life in himself — absolute, independent" — and the Reformed Calvin makes the Son "the fountain of" life who, containing its "fullness," communicates it to all (Psalm 36:9). Ellicott distinguishes the planes: to the Son "in His pre-existent state it was natural, as being equal with the Father," while to the Son who "had emptied Himself" it was given — and Clarke flatly refers the verse to "his character of Messiah, or envoy of God." Cyril of Jerusalem clinches the point doctrinally: "as the Father hath life in Himself, so also hath He given to the Son to have life in Himself" proves that "He who was begotten is God."

The witnesses agree that "life in himself" names underived, self-existent life, the ground of the Son's power to quicken. Barnes contrasts the "living God" with lifeless idols (Acts 14:15; Jeremiah 10:10) and our own life, "breathed" into us and so derivative (Genesis 2:7). Robertson, following Westcott, takes the "as... so" to mark "the fact, not the degree," the Son possessing life as wholly in Himself as the Father. The division concerns the "given." Wesley refers it to "eternal generation," life held "absolute, independent"; Calvin makes the Son "the fountain" who, holding life's "fullness," communicates it to all (Psalm 36:9). Ellicott distributes it across the two states — natural to the pre-existent Son "as being equal with the Father," given to the Son who "had emptied Himself." Cyril of Jerusalem clinches the dogmatic point: the gift of self-existent life proves "that He who was begotten is God."
27:
The clause "because he is the Son of man" as the ground of His "authority to execute judgment" opens a genuine grammatical debate among the witnesses, even as they agree that the office is the Father's gift. The grammatical-historical commentators split over the title. Ellicott and Robertson, with Westcott, stress that the Greek is anarthrous — "a son of man" — differing from the usual Messianic form, and so accents Christ's humanity: "the judge of men must partake of human nature himself." Lightfoot and Clarke read it instead as the Messianic title drawn from Daniel 7:13 — "one like the Son of man... there was given him dominion" — which the rabbis themselves referred to the Messiah. Clarke also records the alternative punctuation that joins the clause to the next verse ("Marvel not at this, because he is the Son of man").

The Latin and Antiochene Catena keeps the doctrine plain: the Father granted the Son power "not only to give life, but also to execute judgment," and Chrysostom explains why Christ dwells so on judgment, resurrection, and life — they are "the most powerful arguments for bringing men over to the faith." The Reformed Calvin reads the "authority" as "rule and government," the Father having "appointed him to be King" over heaven and earth; Wesley puts it simply — He "is appointed to judge mankind because he was made man"; and Cyril of Jerusalem links the title to the willing passion, "the Son of man shall be betrayed, and shall be crucified."

The grammatical debate over "the Son of man" turns on the absent article. Robertson, with Westcott, reads "a son of man" — no articles, "not as the Messiah" — because "the judge of men must partake of human nature himself," and disputes Bernard's view that John here inserts his own reflection. Ellicott agrees the anarthrous form "differs... from the usual term for the Messiah" and recurs only in Revelation 1:13 and 14:14, set "in contrast to the Son of God." Lightfoot and Clarke take the title in its Messianic weight from Daniel 7:13, where "one like the Son of man" receives "dominion, and glory." Beneath the dispute the doctrine is shared: the Antiochene Chrysostom explains that Christ dwells on judgment, resurrection, and life because they are "the most powerful arguments for bringing men over to the faith," and Calvin reads the "authority" as the kingly rule by which the Son governs all.
28:
With "Marvel not at this: for the hour is coming, in which all that are in the graves shall hear his voice," the witnesses agree that the reference now shifts from the spiritual resurrection of v.25 to the bodily resurrection at the last day. The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks the decisive omission — here there is no "and now is" (as in v.25) — so "all that are in the graves" denotes the literally dead. Robertson confirms it from the vocabulary, distinguishing the word for grave (a memorial sepulchre) from a mere burial, and finds Jesus claiming "power to quicken the actual dead at the Last Day," a general bodily resurrection and judgment. Barnes adds that though many have "turned to their native dust," God sees them and can regather their remains, though not necessarily the same particles of matter.

Clarke raises a textual-punctuation point, preferring the reading of the Syriac, Armenian, Chrysostom, Damascenus, and Theophylact that carries "because he is the Son of man" from v.27 into "Marvel not at this" — a reading Theophylact says the common arrangement, ascribed to Paul of Samosata, displaced. The Reformed Calvin meets the objection that Christ "reasons inconclusively" in proving the lesser (spiritual life) from the greater (bodily resurrection): the comparison is not of intrinsic power but of human estimation, since carnal minds "admire nothing but what is outward and visible."

Here the witnesses agree the reference is the bodily resurrection of the last day, marked off from v.25 by the dropping of "and now is." Barnes presses the universality — "all the dead, of every age and nation," though many have "turned to their native dust," for God "can regather their remains," a raising that does not require the same particles of matter. Robertson distinguishes the word for tomb (a memorial sepulchre) from mere burial, and finds Christ claiming "a general judgement and a general bodily resurrection." Clarke presses a punctuation he prefers on the authority of the Syriac, Armenian, Chrysostom, Damascenus, and Theophylact, carrying "because he is the Son of man" from v.27 into "Marvel not." The Reformed Calvin meets the charge that Christ argues "inconclusively" from the greater to the less: the comparison rests not on intrinsic power but on human estimation, since carnal minds "admire nothing but what is outward and visible."
29:
The two outcomes — "the resurrection of life" for those who have done good and "the resurrection of damnation" for those who have done evil — unite the witnesses, who take care to read the "good works" as evidence rather than ground. The grammatical-historical commentators refine the terms. Ellicott prefers "judgment" to "damnation," and notes the rare and deliberate words for "done good" and "practised evil," a double opposition mirroring John 3:20–21. Robertson observes that "resurrection of life" and "resurrection of judgement" occur nowhere else, comparing the "resurrection of the just" at Luke 14:14. Barnes identifies "they that have done good" as the righteous, the friends of Christ (Matthew 25:34–36).

The Reformed Calvin most explicitly guards against a works-righteousness reading: Christ "points out believers by good works," as a tree is known by its fruit (Matthew 7:16; Luke 6:44); the dying robber (Luke 23:42), "born again a new man," shows that the good works praised are the fruit of a renewed life, not its purchase. The Wesleyan Wesley simply glosses "the resurrection of life" as "that resurrection which leads to life everlasting."

The witnesses take pains to read the "good" as evidence, not purchase. Barnes glosses "they that have done good" as the righteous, Christ's friends, who will be raised to "the full enjoyment and perpetual security" of life (cf. Matthew 25:34–36). Ellicott prefers "judgment" to "damnation" and notes the deliberate, rare verbs for doing good and practising evil, a "double opposition" echoing John 3:20–21. Robertson observes the unique phrases "resurrection of life" and "resurrection of judgement," comparing the "resurrection of the just" at Luke 14:14. The Reformed Calvin guards the doctrine most explicitly: Christ "points out believers by good works," as the tree is known by its fruit (Matthew 7:16), and the dying robber (Luke 23:42), "born again a new man," shows the good works praised to be the fruit of a renewed life, never its price. Wesley simply names it the resurrection "which leads to life everlasting."

Gill anchors the "coming forth" in the pattern already seen — as Lazarus came at the word of command (John 11:43) and as the bodies of the saints rose after Christ's own resurrection — so that the future general resurrection is of a piece with the raisings already wrought; the two destinies turn not on the works as merit but on whose voice the dead have heard and obeyed.
30:
Returning to the first person — "I can of mine own self do nothing... I seek not mine own will, but the will of the Father" — the verse reprises v.19, and the witnesses again read it as harmony of will, not subordination of nature. The Latin and Antiochene Catena sets the sense: nothing "unlike to, what the Father wishes" can be seen done by the Son, and "as I hear, I judge" means "as if it were My Father that judged" — it being "impossible He should ever wish any thing but what the Father wished." The grammatical-historical Barnes glosses "as I hear" as the language of one "commissioned... taught" (cf. John 8:28), and Robertson notes the judgment is called "righteous" precisely because its "guiding principle... being the will of the Father." Lightfoot supplies a vivid Jewish parallel: "as I hear, I judge" echoes the custom whereby an inferior judge in doubt "goes up to Jerusalem and takes the determination of the Sanhedrim."

The Reformed Calvin uses the verse to dismiss centuries of overreaction: it is "superfluous" to wrangle here over the eternal Divinity, and the ancients' "anxiety and distress" in refuting Arius was needless, since the holy men rightly replied that "the Son justly claims" equality. The Wesleyan Wesley reads the impossibility positively — "It is impossible I should do any thing separately from my Father" — because "essentially united to him," while Clarke locates the subjection in "the human nature which is the temple of my Divinity" (John 1:14), the pattern of all the Lord's followers. The Puritan Henry adds that the entire agreement of Father and Son means His works "bore witness to all he had said."

The Catena's Latin and Antiochene fathers hold the verse to one will: Chrysostom glosses "as I hear, I judge" as "as if it were My Father that judged," showing "it was impossible He should ever wish any thing but what the Father wished," and Augustine adds that the soul's resurrection and the body's are alike the Father's work in the Son. Barnes reads "as I hear" as the speech of one "commissioned, taught, or sent" (John 8:28), and Robertson notes the discourse returns to the first person, repeating v.19, the judgment called "righteous" precisely because guided by "the will of the Father." Clarke locates the subjection in "the human nature which is the temple of my Divinity" (John 1:14), the believer's pattern. The Puritan Henry hears the whole as Christ declaring "the entire agreement between the Father and the Son," His works bearing "witness to all he had said."
31:
The startling words "If I bear witness of myself, my witness is not true" set the witnesses to reconciling them with John 8:14, where Christ says the opposite, and the dominant solution is the legal sense of "true." The Antiochene Chrysostom, in the Catena, faces the difficulty head-on — "if all this is false, where is our hope of salvation?" — and answers that "true" is said "not with reference to the intrinsic value of His testimony" but to its standing as legal proof. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson supply the law: a man's testimony was not received in his own case (Deuteronomy 19:15; cf. Matthew 18:16), so "true" here means worthy of legal credit; the Puritan Henry agrees that though the self-witness is "infallibly true" (John 8:14), "according to the common rule of judgment among men, you will not admit it."

The Reformed Calvin reads it as spoken "by way of concession" — "if my testimony concerning myself is suspected by you according to the ordinary custom of men, let it go for nothing" — without really detracting from its credit; Wesley makes the same point: "if I alone (which indeed is impossible) ... my testimony is not valid." A dissenting line comes from the Wesleyan Clarke, who reports Bishop Pearce's interrogative rendering — "is not my witness true?" — turning the apparent denial into an affirmation. So the debate is over the form of the harmony: a concession to legal custom (Chrysostom, Henry, Barnes, Calvin, Robertson) or a rhetorical question (Pearce, in Clarke).

The Catena's Chrysostom frames the difficulty squarely — "does not Christ in many places bear witness of Himself?" — and answers that "true" here is said "not with reference to the intrinsic value of His testimony," but to its standing under the rule that a man is no witness in his own cause. Barnes supplies the statute: "the law did not admit a man to testify in his own case, but required two" (Deuteronomy 19:15; cf. Matthew 18:16). The Puritan Henry adds that the self-witness, though "infallibly true" (John 8:14), would not be "admit[ted]... as legal proof." Calvin makes it a "concession" — let it "go for nothing" by their custom — without surrendering its real credit; and the dissenting line, Bishop Pearce's interrogative "is not my witness true?" reported by Clarke, reaches the same harmony with John 8:14 by turning denial into affirmation.
32:
The verse "There is another that beareth witness of me" turns almost entirely on a single question — the identity of the "another" — and here the witnesses genuinely divide. The majority, led by the grammatical-historical commentators, identify the Father. Robertson is firm: "the Father, not the Baptist who is mentioned in verse 33," this being the "continual witness" resumed in vv.36–38 (cf. John 8:17). Ellicott agrees that "the reference to the Baptist is excluded by the words which follow," and that the point lies in "another, different in personality from Himself." Barnes ("that is, God"), Clarke (God witnessing through the prophets), and Wesley ("the Father," v.37) all concur.

Against this stands the Reformed Gill, who argues that the "another" is not the Father — distinctly named later in v.37 — but "another man," namely John the Baptist, taking the "climax, or gradation" of the passage to move from the human witness upward. So the debate is clean: the Father (Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson, Wesley) against John the Baptist (Gill), the former resting its case on the "I know" and the witnesses still to be named.

The grammar reinforces the majority case. Robertson stresses the present participle and present indicative — a "continual witness" that "is bearing witness" — resumed in vv.36–38 and paralleled at John 8:17, and insists this "another" is "the Father, not the Baptist who is mentioned in verse 33." Ellicott finds "the reference to the Baptist... excluded by the words which follow," the point lying in "another, different in personality from Himself" (cf. John 8:50). Barnes ("that is, God"), Clarke, and Wesley all read the Father, looking ahead to v.37. Only the Reformed Gill dissents, urging that the Father is "particularly mentioned" later and that the "climax, or gradation" of the argument moves upward from a human witness — John the Baptist — to the greater witnesses of the works and the Father. The weight of the witnesses, however, rests with the divine reading.

The point at issue, Ellicott adds, is precisely that the witness is borne by "another, different in personality from Himself," which meets the legal objection of v.31 that a man may not testify in his own cause; the very structure of the argument — a second and greater witness — thus tells against Gill's reading and for the Father.
33:
On "Ye sent unto John, and he bare witness unto the truth," the witnesses agree that Christ presses the Jews with a testimony they had themselves solicited and could not honourably disown. The grammatical-historical Ellicott and Robertson mark the perfect tenses — "Ye have sent... he hath borne witness" — so that the Baptist's testimony has "permanent and abiding value" the Sanhedrin must own; the emphatic "ye" stands opposed to the "I" of the next verse, and the object of John's mission was "to bear witness of the Light" (John 1:7; 1:19). Barnes notes that John was "eminent... candid, unambitious, sincere," and that his evidence ought to have satisfied them, though Jesus is willing to adduce higher proof. Clarke lists the marks that make John's witness "unexceptionable" — consulted by Christ's own enemies, free of self-interest, and undaunted.

The Reformed Calvin sharpens the argument ad hominem: the Jews had treated John "as a Prophet of God," and to send to him and then refuse his words convicts them of "their own malice." The Wesleyan Wesley fixes the content of the witness — John testified "that I am the Christ."

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the perfect tenses and the irony they expose. Robertson: the emphatic "ye have sent" makes the Sanhedrin's own deputation (John 1:19–28) a fact they cannot disown, John's witness retaining "permanent and abiding value." Ellicott opposes the emphatic "ye" who "sought human witness" to the "I" of v.34 who "had witness which was divine," recalling that John's whole mission was "to bear witness of the Light" (John 1:7). Clarke lists the marks that made the testimony "unexceptionable" — John was consulted by Christ's own enemies, "free from all self-interest," sincere and undaunted. The Reformed Calvin sharpens the argument into a charge: having owned John "as a Prophet of God," they could not "honorably withhold their belief," and their refusal convicts them of "their own malice." Wesley fixes the content — John testified "that I am the Christ."

Barnes presses that John's testimony alone "ought to have satisfied them," for he was eminent and "impartial," and many of the Pharisees had believed on him; that Christ does not rest His case there, but moves on to the greater witnesses of the works and the Father, shows the appeal to John was a concession to their own procedure rather than the ground of His claim.
34:
The qualification "But I receive not testimony from man: but these things I say, that ye might be saved" draws a single, shared reading — Christ stands above human witness yet stoops to it for their sake. The grammatical-historical commentators set the balance. Robertson catches the emphatic contrast ("I do not receive the witness" simply from a man like John, the "I" sharply against the "ye" of v.33) and the saving purpose of the final clause. Ellicott resolves the apparent tension with v.33: Christ did receive witness from John, but it was "not the witness upon which all was based" — its purpose was to "lead them to Christ Himself." Barnes presses the appeal: it was "evidence of your own seeking," clear and explicit, which, believed, would have saved them.

The Reformed Calvin guards the point precisely: God truly "chose Christ to be a witness to him," and Christ avails himself of John's testimony "not because he needs it, but so far as it is advantageous to us" for confirmation, just as the disciples would later be His witnesses (Acts 1:8). The Wesleyan Wesley draws the pathos — "so really and seriously did he will their salvation," yet most "died in their sins" — and Cyril of Jerusalem echoes the principle that one should "receive not testimony from man" apart from the prophets and Scripture.

The grammatical-historical commentators sharpen both the contrast and the purpose. Robertson reads "I do not receive the witness" simply "from a man (like John)," the emphatic "I" set against the "ye" of v.33, recalling that even Nicodemus would not receive Christ's witness (John 3:11). Barnes presses the appeal home — John's testimony was "clear, full, explicit," and "evidence of your own seeking," which, believed, would have led them to the Messiah and "been saved." Ellicott resolves the tension with v.33: Christ did receive witness from John, "but this was not the witness upon which all was based," its purpose being to "lead them to Christ Himself."
