Calvin holds the same balance — Christ "avails himself" of John's testimony "not because he needs it, but so far as it is advantageous to us" — and Wesley draws the pathos that He "really and seriously" willed a salvation most of them refused.
35:
The tribute to the Baptist — "He was a burning and a shining light: and ye were willing for a season to rejoice in his light" — gathers a firm consensus, sharpened by a point of vocabulary. The grammatical-historical commentators insist the word is lamp, not light. Clarke and Barnes note that the Greek is luchnos — "a burning and a shining Lamp," a kindled lamp, not to phos, the sun — for John "was not the Light of the world" but the lamp to shine "till the sun should arise" (cf. John 1:8). Ellicott renders it "the lamp that is lighted and... giveth light," and Robertson observes that the past tense "was" implies John is now in prison, his active ministry over, the same word luchnos later naming Christ as the Lamp of the New Jerusalem (Revelation 21:23). Lightfoot grounds the image in Jewish idiom, where a famous teacher is called "the candle of the law."

The Reformed Calvin turns the verse to indictment: God "kindled a lamp before their eyes," which proves their blindness "voluntary"; and the phrase "for a season" exposes their fickleness — a brief, shallow rejoicing in John's brightness. The Wesleyan Wesley reads the lamp inwardly and outwardly — "inwardly burning with love and zeal, outwardly shining in all holiness" — and notes their willingness lasted only "a short time."

The vocabulary point governs the verse. Clarke insists the Greek is "a burning and a shining Lamp," not to phos, "the light of the world, i.e. the sun," for John was "a lamp, to give a clear and steady light till the sun should arise" (cf. John 1:8). Robertson notes the past tense "was" implies John is now imprisoned, his ministry over, and that the same word names Christ as the "Lamp" of the New Jerusalem (Revelation 21:23), distinct from the torch of Matthew 25:1. Lightfoot grounds the figure in the rabbinic title "the candle of the law." The Reformed Calvin turns it to indictment — God "kindled a lamp before their eyes," so their blindness is "voluntary error," and the "for a season" exposes a fickle, short-lived zeal. Wesley reads the lamp inwardly and outwardly: "inwardly burning with love and zeal, outwardly shining in all holiness."
36:
Christ's claim to "greater witness than that of John" — the works the Father gave Him to finish — is read uniformly as the decisive evidence. The grammatical-historical commentators are careful with the comparison. Gill and Robertson note it is greater than John's witness, not greater than John himself; Robertson renders "I have the witness greater than John's" and marks the purpose-clause "to accomplish" (the same idiom as John 4:34), sensing how keenly "Jesus felt the task laid on" Him. Barnes identifies the works as the miracles — healing and raising — which "bear witness" that the Father sent Him, the verb "to finish" looking ahead to the cry "It is finished" (John 19:30). Ellicott widens the scope: these "works" are "not confined to what we speak of as miracles," and the verse returns to the Father's witness of v.32.

Clarke concedes John was "the lamp of Israel," a witness more than any prophet, yet insists Christ is "not obliged to depend on his testimony alone," having the greater witness of God Himself. The Reformed Calvin draws the same line: having shown how the Jews "corrupted the gift of God" in John, Christ "sent them to his Father," whose works "attest by miracles that I am his Son."

The grammatical-historical commentators secure the comparison and the term. Gill and Robertson note the witness is greater than John's testimony, not greater than John himself, Robertson rendering it "I have the witness greater than John's" and marking the purpose-clause "to accomplish" (the idiom of John 4:34), sensing how "keenly" Jesus felt the appointed task. Barnes names the works as the miracles that "bear witness" the Father sent Him, the verb "to finish" reaching toward the cry "It is finished" (John 19:30). Ellicott widens the scope — these "works" are "not confined to what we speak of as miracles" — and returns the thought to the Father's witness of v.32. The Reformed Calvin completes the move: having shown how the Jews "corrupted the gift of God" in John, Christ "sent them to his Father," whose works attest "that I am his Son."
37:
The Father's own witness — "the Father himself... hath borne witness of me" — together with the rebuke "Ye have neither heard his voice at any time, nor seen his shape," sets the witnesses debating which testimony is meant. The Reformed Calvin urges the broadest reference: the perfect tense ("hath borne witness") shows the Father did "not come forward as an unknown person," having "long ago" distinguished the Son "whenever" He spoke in the Law and the Prophets — so the witness is not narrowed to one event. Robertson agrees this is the "direct witness of the Father, besides the indirect witness of the works," and explicitly excludes the voice at the baptism, the transfiguration, and John 12:28.

Against the broad reading, the Wesleyan Wesley fixes on the baptism — "He hath testified of me — namely at my baptism" (Matthew 3:17) — adding the pointed aside, "I speak not of my supposed father Joseph; ye are utter strangers to him of whom I speak." Clarke mediates: the Father bore witness "by his prophets," and the clause "ye have neither heard his voice" is a parenthesis (so Bishop Pearce) meaning God spoke not audibly but through the prophets, though "lately" He had added the voice from heaven. Barnes lists the options — miracles, the baptism (Matthew 3:17), the prophecies — and declines to choose, suspecting "all." So the debate is between the whole Old Testament testimony (Calvin, Robertson) and the specific heavenly voice (Wesley), with Clarke and Barnes spreading the reference across both.

The division over the Father's witness sharpens here. The Reformed Calvin rejects narrowing it "to the voice which was heard at his baptism," for the perfect tense ("hath borne witness") shows the Father "long ago" distinguished the Son "whenever" He spoke in the Law and Prophets. Robertson likewise excludes the baptismal voice (Mark 1:11), the transfiguration, and John 12:28, taking this as the Father's "direct witness... besides the indirect witness of the works." Against the broad reading, Wesley fixes on the baptism — adding the aside, "I speak not of my supposed father Joseph; ye are utter strangers to him of whom I speak" — and Clarke treats "ye have neither heard his voice" as a parenthesis (after Bishop Pearce): God spoke "by the mouths of his prophets," though "lately" adding the heavenly voice. Barnes lists miracles, baptism (Matthew 3:17), and prophecy together, declining to choose among them.
38:
The charge "ye have not his word abiding in you: for whom he hath sent, him ye believe not" is read with one voice: the proof that God's word has no home in them is their rejection of the Sent One. The grammatical-historical Ellicott dwells on the striking image of "the word taking up its abode in the mind, and forming the mind in which it dwells" — a Johannine thought (John 15:7; 1 John 2:14) — and the indictment that they "locked it up with sacred care in ark and synagogue," where it "found no home in their inmost life." Robertson notes that God's word had come to them through the prophets, yet they "reject both Jesus and God's word." Barnes frames it as the third count against them: they neither obeyed God's command, nor regarded His manifestations, nor yielded to the written revelation.

The Wesleyan Clarke turns it pastoral — they held the Scriptures to be of God yet "do not let them take hold of your hearts," the word "in your mouth, but not in your mind" — and contrasts the Psalmist who hid God's word in his heart (Psalm 119:11). The Reformed Calvin makes the same diagnosis structurally: the word "has no place among the Jews, because they do not receive the Son of God, on whom it everywhere bestows commendation" — for Moses and the prophets meant only "to invite all men" to the Son. Wesley states the converse: "all who believe have the word of the Father... abiding in them."

The witnesses press the charge that the proof of an absent word is a rejected Messiah. Barnes counts it the third in a sequence — they had not obeyed God's command, not regarded His manifestations, and now did not "yield to what he had said in the revelation of the Old Testament." Robertson notes the word had come "through the centuries by the prophets," yet they "reject both Jesus and God's word" (cf. John 10:35; 1 John 1:10). Clarke turns it pastoral — they held the Scriptures "to be of God" yet kept the word "in your mouth, but not in your mind," unlike the Psalmist who hid it "in my heart" (Psalm 119:11). Calvin diagnoses the cause structurally: "the heavenly doctrine has no place among the Jews, because they do not receive the Son of God, on whom it everywhere bestows commendation" — Moses and the prophets having meant only to "invite all men" to the Son.
39:
The famous words "Search the scriptures... they are they which testify of me" generate the chapter's sharpest grammatical debate — whether the verb is imperative (a command, "Search") or indicative (a description, "Ye search") — even as all agree on the substance, that the Scriptures bear witness to Christ. The grammatical-historical commentators lean indicative. Lightfoot reads "Ye search the Scriptures," Clarke insists the indicative alone accords with v.40, and Robertson, granting the form is ambiguous, judges that the reason "because ye think" (plainly indicative) "supports the indicative," Jesus arguing "on their own premises." Gill allows the same rendering. Barnes, while keeping the imperative tone, mines the verb's force — to "search diligently or anxiously," as miners search for ore (Job 28:3).

The Wesleyan Wesley reads it imperatively — "a plain command to all men" — and the Puritan Henry holds both together: "Ye do search the Scriptures, and ye do well to do so," yet warns it is "possible for men to be very studious in the letter... yet to be strangers to its power." The Reformed Calvin presses that point: Christ does not "absolutely blame them for seeking life in" the Scriptures, which were given for that end, but for resting in "the dead letter" while missing the Christ to whom they testify. The Antiochene Chrysostom exhorts to "make great account of spiritual things," and Origen, more obliquely, threads the witness of John and the prophets to the Word.

The imperative-or-indicative crux runs, Ellicott admits, "through the whole history of New Testament exposition," the best judges being "almost equally divided." The grammatical-historical weight leans indicative: Lightfoot reads "Ye search the Scriptures," Clarke holds the imperative "can never accord well with the following verse" (v.40), and Robertson, granting the form ambiguous, finds the reason "because ye think" — plainly indicative — decisive, Jesus arguing "on their own premises." Barnes, keeping the imperative tone, mines the verb's intensity: to search as miners search for ore (Job 28:3), or as a hound traces a scent. Wesley reads it as "a plain command to all men," the Scriptures showing "the way to eternal life" and testifying of Christ. The Puritan Henry and the Reformed Calvin meet in the substance: it is no fault to "seek life" in the Scriptures, only to rest in "the dead letter" while missing the Christ to whom they point.
40:
The verdict "And ye will not come to me, that ye might have life" is read by every witness as locating the refusal in the will, not the understanding. The grammatical-historical Ellicott states it plainly: "The real hindrance is once more traced to the will. It is moral, not intellectual" — "if ye were willing to come, ye should really have" the life they thought they possessed. Robertson hears the Johannine "and yet" and calls it "the tragedy of the rejection of Messiah by the Messianic race," echoing John 1:11 and the lament of Matthew 23:37, since "men loved darkness rather than light" (John 3:19); the offer of life is the very purpose of the Gospel (John 20:31).

Gill distinguishes the "coming" from a merely bodily approach — many came corporeally, for loaves or to cavil — and makes it the spiritual coming of faith. Barnes draws the application that life is in Christ alone, to be had by coming "as lost sinners." The Reformed Calvin completes the chapter's indictment: "nothing but their own malice" hinders them; they were "willfully blind," intentionally fleeing the light and even seeking "to extinguish the sun by the darkness of their unbelief."

The witnesses locate the whole tragedy in the will. Ellicott: "The real hindrance is once more traced to the will. It is moral, not intellectual" — had they been "willing to come," they "should really have" the life they imagined themselves to possess. Robertson hears the Johannine "and yet" and names it "the tragedy of the rejection of Messiah by the Messianic race," echoing John 1:11 and the lament of Matthew 23:37 — "and ye would not" — since "men loved darkness rather than light" (John 3:19); this very offer of life is the Gospel's stated purpose (John 20:31). Gill insists the "coming" is no bodily approach — many came for loaves or to cavil — but the coming of faith. Calvin closes the indictment: "nothing but their own malice" hinders them, "willfully blind," seeking "to extinguish the sun by the darkness of their unbelief."
41:
The disclaimer "I receive not honour from men" opens the discourse's closing diagnosis of unbelief, and the witnesses agree it is no expression of wounded pride but a setting-aside of self-interest. The Antiochene Chrysostom, in the Catena, supplies the motive: having appealed to John, to God, and to His works, Christ guards against the suspicion of "a desire for human glory" — "I do not want it. My nature is not such as to want that glory, which comes from men." The grammatical-historical Robertson confirms it: "this is not wounded pride, for ambition is not Christ's motive," and He stands wholly unlike the Jews who loved "the praise of men" (v.44; John 12:43), as Paul too sought no glory from men (1 Thessalonians 2:6). Ellicott prefers the rendering "glory," and reads the verse as beginning Christ's account of "the true cause of their incredulity."

Barnes and Clarke draw the same purpose: Christ speaks not from any craving for applause but to explain why they do not believe — and Barnes leaps ahead to the root He will name, that "you have not the love of God in you." The Reformed Calvin makes the point with characteristic height: Christ is "too great to depend on the opinions of men," so that "the malignity of the whole world can take nothing from him."

The Catena's fathers supply the motive for the disclaimer. Chrysostom: lest those who saw Him appeal to John, to God, and to His works should "suspect Him of a desire for human glory," Christ says "I do not want it," for "if the Son receives no addition from the light of a candle, much more am not I in want of human glory." Robertson agrees this is "not wounded pride, for ambition is not Christ's motive," and contrasts Him with the Jews who loved "the praise of men" (John 12:43), even as Paul "did not seek glory from men" (1 Thessalonians 2:6). Ellicott reads "glory" as the better word and marks the verse as opening Christ's diagnosis of "the true cause of their incredulity" (vv.41–47). The Reformed Calvin sets the tone for that diagnosis — Christ is "too great to depend on the opinions of men," and exalts Himself above their contempt only to refute their calumny.
42:
The piercing "I know you, that ye have not the love of God in you" is heard by all the witnesses as Christ laying bare the true root of their rejection. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the perfect — "I have come to know and still know" — the knowledge "of personal experience" (John 2:24), and parses "the love of God" as an objective genitive, "the love toward God" (as at Luke 11:42). Ellicott draws out the irony: the very principle that excludes the seeking of honour from men is the love of God, the "first precept of the Law" (Deuteronomy 6:4–9; 11:13–21) which every Jew bound on brow and arm in the phylacteries (Matthew 23:5) — and yet "had... without," not within.

The Wesleyan Clarke turns it directly against their pretended zeal: "Don't say that you oppose me through zeal for God's honour" — "ye have neither love for his name, nor zeal for his glory," but "incorrigible ignorance, and malicious jealousy." Wesley softens the tone without blunting it: "with this ray he pierces the hearts of the hearers," yet "this doubtless he spake with the tenderest compassion." The Reformed Calvin makes the love of God "the summary or recapitulation of the Law" (Deuteronomy 6:5) — its absence leaving "no desire to obey him."

Robertson grounds the verse in grammar and lexicon: the perfect "I have come to know and still know" is the knowledge "of personal experience" (John 2:24), and "the love of God" is an objective genitive — "the love toward God" — the same phrase as Luke 11:42 and common in 1 John. Ellicott draws out the bitter irony that the principle excluding the love of human honour is the love of God, the "first precept of the Law" (Deuteronomy 6:4–9), which the Pharisees "made broad" on their phylacteries (Matthew 23:5) yet "had... without" rather than within. Calvin makes that love "the summary or recapitulation of the Law" (Deuteronomy 6:5), its absence leaving "no desire to obey." Wesley notes the tenderness beneath the thrust — "with this ray he pierces the hearts," yet "with the tenderest compassion."
43:
The contrast "I am come in my Father's name, and ye receive me not: if another shall come in his own name, him ye will receive" draws a shared reading — their rejection of the true exposes a readiness for the false — with one debate over the identity of "another." The grammatical-historical commentators note the deliberate antithesis: Christ comes representing the Father, "guided only by His will, doing only His work" (Ellicott; John 4:34), and Robertson observes how often John records the "Name" of the Father on Christ's lips (John 17:6, 11). On "another," Barnes and Clarke read false teachers generally — the Jews, divided between the schools of Hillel and Shammai, were prone to follow a self-commissioned master, and Clarke cites Josephus on the false messiahs who arose "in his own name."

The patristic and grammatical voices press toward a sharper referent — the Antichrist. Cyril of Jerusalem reads it as prophecy: "the sons of the Jews... rejected the true Messiah, and wait for the deceiver," so that "him ye will receive" finds Christ "true," and Robertson too hears the antichrist or false messiahs. The Reformed Calvin generalizes the principle against all false claimants — the false prophets, and "the Pope," boast the title while Christ "means the reality, and not a hypocritical pretense," proving His own commission by faithfully executing it. So the debate is one of scope: the historical false messiahs (Barnes, Clarke) or the eschatological Antichrist (Cyril, Robertson).

The grammatical and historical detail fills out the antithesis. Robertson notes that John records the "Name" of the Father on Christ's lips seven times (John 17:6, 11, 12), and reads "if another come" as a real future possibility — the antichrist or the false messiahs. Clarke grounds the warning in rabbinic custom, where a teacher must "support his doctrine by the authority" of the eminent, and cites Josephus on the impostors who arose "in his own name." Ellicott traces the rejection to its root, the "absence of love": had they loved the Father, "they must have received and reverenced His Son" (Matthew 21:37). Cyril of Jerusalem reads it as fulfilled prophecy — having "set at nought Him that came," they "wait for the deceiver, themselves deceived," proving the Saviour true. Calvin universalizes it against every false claimant who boasts the title while Christ "means the reality."
44:
The challenge "How can ye believe, which receive honour one of another, and seek not the honour that cometh from God only?" draws a strong consensus: the craving for human honour makes faith impossible. The Wesleyan Clarke names it bluntly — "the grand obstacle to the salvation of the scribes and Pharisees was their pride, vanity, and self-love"; they "lived on each other's praise," and rather than forfeit reputation "chose... to lose their souls," which Clarke calls "the ruin of millions." The grammatical-historical Ellicott and Robertson sharpen the emphatic "ye": it is "not possible that ye should believe" while in this disposition, for to believe "would be to give up your whole present life"; Robertson adds that they were "not true Jews" (Romans 2:29) who cared for the glory of God. Barnes traces their rejection to the same source — expecting "a Messiah of pomp and splendour," they would not submit to one so lowly.

The Reformed Calvin diagnoses that "ambition has deprived them of sound judgment," the priests and scribes "swelled with pride." The Wesleyan Wesley adds a pointed historical barb: if the feast was Pentecost, their sermons were then "full of the praises of the law, and of the people to whom it was given" — making Christ's exposure of their love of human honour especially "mortifying."

The witnesses press a single diagnosis: ambition makes faith impossible. Ellicott reads the emphatic pronoun — "It is not possible that ye should believe in Me, as our whole position is entirely different" — for to believe "would be to give up your whole present life." Robertson adds that they were "not true Jews" (Romans 2:29) who cared for the glory of God, preferring "the praise of men" like the rulers who feared to confess Christ (John 12:43). Barnes traces their rejection to the expectation of "a Messiah of pomp and splendour," unworthy of submission to one so lowly. Clarke calls pride "the ruin of millions" who "would be religious, if religion and worldly honor were connected." Wesley adds the mortifying setting: if this was Pentecost, their sermons were then "full of the praises of the law, and of the people to whom it was given" — the very self-glory Christ here exposes.
45:
Christ's turn — "Do not think that I will accuse you to the Father: there is one that accuseth you, even Moses, in whom ye trust" — unites the witnesses on the irony that their own trusted lawgiver is their accuser. The grammatical-historical Robertson marks the legal vocabulary — the verb is "to bring an accusation in court" — and the prohibition "Think not." Ellicott catches the tenderness beneath the severity: His words "must have cut to the very quick," yet "love cannot accuse"; He is a judge only because "love must judge hatred, and light must judge darkness, by revealing it" (John 3:19). Barnes explains that Moses "wrote of the Messiah... and commanded them to hear him," so their unbelief turns his witness into an indictment, and Clarke adds that Christ could justly accuse them of breaking "the whole law," but need not, since Moses does it.

The Puritan Henry generalizes the warning: many "trust in some form of doctrines or some parties," no more entering their real meaning "than the Jews did into those of Moses." The Reformed Calvin makes it a model for dealing with the hardened — when instruction and warning fail, "they must be summoned to the judgment-seat of God" — against those who "amuse themselves at their ease with empty flatteries" of a God they oppose. The Wesleyan Wesley fixes the mode of accusation: Moses accuses them "by his writings."

The witnesses agree the irony is exact: their trusted lawgiver is their accuser. Robertson marks the legal vocabulary — "to bring an accusation in court" — and the prohibition "Think not." Barnes explains that Moses "wrote of the Messiah, clearly foretold his coming, and commanded them to hear him," so their unbelief makes his witness an indictment. Ellicott catches the tenderness within the severity: the words "must have cut to the very quick," yet "Love cannot accuse" — Christ is "a judge" only because "love must judge hatred, and light must judge darkness, by revealing it" (John 3:19). The Reformed Calvin makes it a method for the hardened: when warning fails, "they must be summoned to the judgment-seat of God," against those who "amuse themselves... with empty flatteries" of a God they oppose. The Puritan Henry generalizes it — many "trust in some form of doctrines or some parties" without entering their meaning, as the Jews never entered Moses'.
46:
The home-thrust "had ye believed Moses, ye would have believed me: for he wrote of me" is read by every witness as proof that their professed trust in Moses was hollow. The grammatical-historical Robertson identifies the grammar — a second-class condition, "determined as unfulfilled" — and calls it "a home-thrust, proving that they did not really believe Moses," who wrote of Christ in the promised prophet (Deuteronomy 18:18, cited by Peter at Acts 3:22 and Stephen at Acts 7:37), the brazen serpent (John 3:14), and Abraham's foresight (John 8:56). Clarke and Barnes catalogue the passages — the Shiloh of Genesis 49:10, the prophet of Deuteronomy 18:15, the seed of Genesis 22:18 (cf. Acts 26:22) — and the "multitude of symbols and figures" by which Moses pointed to the Messiah.

Ellicott deepens the diagnosis: the "present incredulity springs from that of the past," for had they truly believed Moses they would have seen in "the whole spirit of the Pentateuch a manifestation of God" — their very word for law, Thorah, meaning "instruction" — but they rested in the surface. The Reformed Calvin adds that ministers of the word "ought to be ready to defend it against despisers," and the Wesleyan Wesley sums it: Moses "wrote of me — every where; in all his writings."

The grammar carries the thrust. Robertson identifies a second-class condition "determined as unfulfilled," a "home-thrust, proving that they did not really believe Moses" — who wrote of Christ in the promised prophet (Deuteronomy 18:18, cited at Acts 3:22 and 7:37), the lifted serpent (John 3:14), and Abraham's joy (John 8:56). Clarke and Barnes gather the texts — the Shiloh of Genesis 49:10, the prophet "like unto thee" of Deuteronomy 18:15, the seed of Genesis 22:18 — and the "multitude of symbols and figures" by which Moses points to the Messiah. Ellicott deepens it: the "present incredulity springs from that of the past," for the very word Thorah means "instruction," and had they believed it they would have seen "a manifestation of God" beneath worship, sacrifice, and priesthood, rather than resting on "what the senses could know."
47:
The closing question — "But if ye believe not his writings, how shall ye believe my words?" — is read by the witnesses as the chapter's final, compounding indictment, and the grammatical-historical commentators sharpen its logic. Robertson sets it beside the parallel argument of Luke 16:31 ("if they hear not Moses and the prophets, neither will they be persuaded though one rose from the dead"), and contrasts the writings (the permanent letter) with the spoken words, the authority of Moses being "the greatest of all for Jews." Ellicott locates the stress differently — "not between 'writings' and 'words,' but between 'his' and 'My'" — yet reaches the same end: if they would not believe Moses, what "ground of hope is left" that they will believe Christ? Barnes draws the abiding lessons — that many "profess to believe the Bible" yet have "no regard for it when it crosses their own... inclinations," and that the Old Testament prophecies establish that Jesus is the Messiah.

Clarke makes the rejection moral rather than evidential — if they "lay not to heart" what Moses' words authorize, they will the less believe words "against which ye have taken up the most ungrounded prejudice." The Reformed Calvin resolves the apparent puzzle that Christ "claim[s] less authority for himself than for Moses": He merely "accommodates his discourse" to hearers for whom "the authority of the Law was... held sacred," and the contrast only "aggravate[s] their unbelief," who reject the very truth of God recorded by the lawgiver they revere.

Robertson sets the saying beside the parallel of Luke 16:31 — "if they hear not Moses and the prophets, neither will they be persuaded though one rose from the dead" — and contrasts the permanent writings with the spoken words, observing that "the authority of Moses was the greatest of all for Jews." Ellicott locates the emphasis differently, "not between 'writings' and 'words,' but between 'his' and 'My,'" yet reaches the same end: unbelief in the lesser forecloses belief in the greater, and "what ground of hope is left?" Barnes draws the abiding warnings — that many "profess to believe the Bible" yet have "no regard for it when it crosses their own... inclinations," and that the Old Testament prophecies are given to establish "that Jesus is the Messiah." The Reformed Calvin resolves the apparent puzzle that Christ claims "less authority for himself than for Moses": it is "accommodation" to hearers who held the Law sacred, and the contrast only "aggravate[s] their unbelief."
